title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Data Context

World Health Organization (WHO)
We are exploring measles and rubella cases across the world. The data was collected and published through the World Health Organization (WHO) portal, but for ease of access, scripts to access the data were provided by tidytuesday.
WHO has published monthly measles and rubella data from 193 countries from June 2012 to 2025. The data is provisional and is early case reports by WHO member countries collected using each countries national surveillance systems. The data contains several types of case confirmations (suspected, clinical, epidemiologically linked, and lab confirmed) as well as population count data in the yearly data set. The data was collected to monitor measles and rubella cases worldwide in order to support public health decision making and vaccination strategies.


Code
#needed libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(rvest)
library(here)
library(broom)
library(ggridges)
library(scales)

# reading in from github
cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')

# need to do some cleaning for the webscraping process
cases_month2 <- cases_month |>
  mutate(month_abb = recode_values(month, 
                        1 ~ "Jan",
                        2 ~ "Feb",
                        3 ~ "Mar",
                        4 ~ "Apr",
                        5 ~ "May",
                        6 ~ "Jun",
                        7 ~ "Jul",
                        8 ~ "Aug",
                        9 ~ "Sep",
                        10 ~ "Oct",
                        11 ~ "Nov",
                        12 ~ "Dec"),
      country_scrape = str_to_lower(country),
      country_scrape = str_replace_all(country_scrape, " ", "-"))




Weather and Climate Data
To investigate the relationship between seasonal patterns and measles cases, we’ve web scraped data provided by WeatherandClimate. WeatherAndClimate is an online climate zone and data finder for 80,000+ locations in the world based on Köppen climate classification. WeatherAndClimate does not disclose the exact source or data collection method. They provide historical records dating to 1980, but we will scrape mean monthly temperature and precipitation data across this time period. The data was collected to monitor Earth’s climate systems which have broad implications on socio-economic conditions.


Code
country_vec <- c(unique(cases_month2$country_scrape))

grab_weather <- function(country) {
   base_url <- "https://weatherandclimate.com/"
   climate_url <- str_c(base_url,country) 
   
   result <- tryCatch({
       read_html(climate_url)
     }, error = function(e) {
       message("Failed for ", country, e$message)
       NULL
     })
 
   if (is.null(result)) {
     return(NULL) 
   } else {
     result |>
       html_element(".tb6") |>
       html_table() |>
       pivot_longer(col = Jan:Dec, names_to = "month", values_to = "stat") |> 
       filter(Month %in% c("Average precipitation mm (inches)", "Daily mean °C (°F)")) |>
       select(!Year) |>
       pivot_wider(names_from = "Month", values_from = "stat") |>
       rename(mean_temp = 2,
          mean_precip = 3) |>
       mutate(mean_temp = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_temp, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             mean_precip = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_precip, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             country = country) #got the regex case from AI looks for values in the ()
 
   }
 }
 
 scraped_weather <- map(.x = country_vec, .f = grab_weather) |>
   bind_rows(.id = "id")
 

# save for loading more than once
# write.csv(scraped_weather, "./site/data/weather-scrape.csv")




Country Demographic Dataset
The Country Demographic Dataset is taken from API Ninja’s Country API, which provides geographic, demographic, and economic statistics about every country in the world. API Ninjas publishes the data through its Country API, but their documentation does not specify the original primary collector of each statistic. Variables in the dataset include: population, GPD, surface area, unemployment, homicide rate, infant mortality, fertility, and more.
The observational unit in this dataset is a country, where each row represents one country and each column represents a country-level statistic. The exact dates at which country statistics were originally collected are not stated in the API documentation. Thus, the data used in this analysis represent the values returned by the API at the time we originally accessed it.
The data was collected for this project by sending requests to the API Ninjas Country API and retrieving the given JSON objects. Each of these JSON objects contains country level statistics, which were then put into a dataset where each country became one observation and each statistic became a variable/column.
The data was collected because it allows us to make comparisons between a country’s economic, demographic, or geographic characteristics and how prevalent measles is in those countries. We hypothesize that such factors are associated with changes in measles cases and have retrieved them from this API to assess our hypothesis.


Code
country_names <- unique(cases_year$country)

api_key <- "hmz9wf1cpAko2WM4Fz3oPInaZIt44Uf9uh3VFfxD"

base_url <- "https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/country"

get_country_demo <- function(country_name){
  GET(url = base_url,
      add_headers('X-API-Key' = api_key),
      query = list(name = country_name)) |>
  content("text", encoding = "utf-8") |>
  fromJSON() |>
  as.data.frame()
}

country_demographic <- map_dfr(country_names, get_country_demo)
# after the join... 2756 of the 22,780 datapoints don't have temp or precip data
# 19 of the 173 countries failed in the iteration
# cleaning script to not need api calls
# country_demographic_clean <- country_demographic |>
#   unnest(currency, names_sep = "_")
# 
# write_csv(country_demographic_clean, "data/country_demographic.csv")




Final Dataset


Code
api_load <- read.csv("./data/country_demographic.csv")
# load csv climate data
scraped_data <- read.csv(here("data", "weather-scrape.csv")) 

cases_month3 <- left_join(cases_month2, scraped_data, by = c("month_abb" = "month",  "country_scrape" = "country" ), 
                          relationship = "many-to-many")
meta_data <- left_join(cases_month3,
                       api_load,
                       by = c("country" = "name"),
                       relationship = "many-to-many")


Our final dataset is the three previous datasets joined together. The web-scraped data was joined onto the measles case data by month and by country. The API fetched data was joined onto the result of the previous join by country. Thus, we have a single dataset containing statistics on measles cases, countries, and environmental factors.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Research Questions

Climate, Geography and Measles Rates
Our first analysis aims to isolate trends in measles rates across climatic factors, natural resources and geography. We aim to answer the following question:

How do climate and geography relate to measles and rubella cases across the world?

And more specifically:

Are there seasonal patterns in measles outbreaks across different regions?
How are climate, natural resources and emissions related to measles/rubella cases?
How are total measles cases spatially distributed throughout the world’s regions?

To better understand the relationship between climate and measles/rubella cases, we will explore monthly mean lab confirmed measles/rubella cases in the northern and southern hemisphere. We will ignore changes across years, and focus on monthly variation. We will compare India and Indonesia, who are both in the top 10 countries with the worst measles outbreaks as of 2023.


Code
# Use of unmodified column, custom function
monthly_sums <- function(df, group, countries) {
  if (is.data.frame(df)) {
    df |>
      filter(country %in% c(countries)) |>
      group_by({{group}}, country) |>
      summarize(
                `Mean Measles Cases` = mean(measles_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                `Mean Rubella Cases` = mean(rubella_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                .groups = "drop") |>
      mutate(month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
      arrange(month_abb) |>
      pivot_longer(cols = c(`Mean Measles Cases`, `Mean Rubella Cases`),
                   names_to =  "Statistic",
                   values_to = "value") |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = month_abb,
                  values_from = value) |>
      group_by(country) |>
      gt() |>
      tab_style(
        style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
        locations = list(cells_column_labels(), cells_row_groups())) |> 
      tab_header(
        title = md("Mean Lab Confirmed Measles and Rubella Cases Per Month")) |>
      tab_source_note(
        source_note = md("*Table 1: Mean monthly lab-confirmed measles and rubella cases over the course of the study (2012-2025).*")) |>
      fmt_number(
        columns  = where(is.numeric),
        decimals = 1) |>
      data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
  } else {
  stop('Must input a dataframe')
  }
}

monthly_sums(df = meta_data, 
             group = month_abb,
            countries = c("Indonesia" , "India"))

From our analysis we see a strong relationship between month and measles/rubella cases. This confirms the long held notion of a northern hemisphere “flu season” during the winter. The relationship in Indonesia is more subtle and may be indicative of their tropical climate.
Next, we investigate how climate, natural resources and emissions are related to measles/rubella cases.


Code
library(corrr)
make_titles <- function(x){
  # expects a character or factor as an input
  stopifnot(is.character(x) | is.factor(x))
  
  # remove the units at the end of the name
  str_remove(x, pattern = "(g|mm)$") |> 
  # replace all _ with spaces
  str_replace_all(pattern = "_", replacement = " ") |> 
  # remove extra whitespace on left 
  str_trim(side = "both") |> 
  # convert the first letter of each word to upper case
  str_to_title()
}

meta_data |>
  select(month, measles_total, measles_epi_linked, rubella_total, 
         rubella_epi_linked, mean_temp, mean_precip, surface_area,
         forested_area, co2_emissions, threatened_species, internet_users) |>
  correlate(method = "pearson",
            use = "pairwise.complete.obs") |>
  rename(term1 = term) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = -term1,
               names_to = "term2",
               values_to = "correlation") |>
  filter(term1 != term2) |>
  mutate(
    across(.cols = c(term1, term2),
           .fns = ~ make_titles(.x)
           )
    ) |>
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = term1,
                       y = term2,
                       fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "",
       title = "Correlation Between Measles/Rubella Cases and Biological Factors",
       fill = "Correlation"
       ) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B")





Figure 1: Correlation matrix showing the relationship between climate, natural resources and emissions data and measles/rubella cases.




When comparing climate and natural resources, we see expected results such as a positive correlation between mean precipitation and forested area. We also see a positive correlation between CO₂ emissions and measles/rubella cases (Figure 1) — a surprising finding that likely reflects population and industrial scale rather than a direct biological link.
Finally, we investigate spatial differences in measles cases from last year and compare the distributions of total measles cases across world regions (Figure 2).


Code
regions_df <- cases_year |>
  mutate(region = recode_values(region,
                    "EMRO" ~ "Eastern Mediterranean",
                    "AFRO" ~ "Africa",
                    "EURO" ~ "Europe",
                    "SEARO" ~ "South-East Asia",
                    "AMRO" ~ "Americas",
                    "WPRO" ~ "Western Pacific"))

region_burden <- function(region_name) {
  regions_df |>
    filter(year == 2025, region == region_name) |>
    mutate(measles_total = as.numeric(measles_total)) |>
    summarize(
      region = region_name,
      total_cases = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
}

regions <- unique(regions_df$region)

map(regions, region_burden) |>
  bind_rows(.id="id") |> 
  mutate(pct_global = round(total_cases / sum(total_cases) * 100, 1)) |>
  arrange(desc(total_cases)) |>
  select(!id) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Regional Measles Burden**"),
    subtitle = "2025 Contribution to Global Total"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    region      = "Region",
    total_cases = "Total Cases",
    pct_global  = "% of Global Total"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_cases, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = pct_global,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern  = "{x}%"
    ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("*Table 2: Total measles cases and share of global burden by WHO region, 2025.*")
  ) |>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )

Code
regions_df |>
  filter(measles_total <= 5000) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_total, y = fct_reorder(region, measles_total, 
                                                .desc = FALSE), )) +
  geom_density_ridges(aes(fill = region),
                      alpha = 0.5, scale = 1,
                      show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Total Measles Cases per Year", 
       y = "", 
       title = "Comparison of Measles Cases across World Regions"
       ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "B")





Figure 2: Comparison of the distribution of measles cases per year across world regions.




The density ridge plot spans the full dataset (2012-2025) and so reflects long-term regional measles rates, which differ from the 2025 snapshot in Table 2. Notably, the regions with the largest 2025 share are not always those with the heaviest long-run distributions — a shift that points to outbreak dynamics a deeper temporal analysis could unpack.


Measles Cases and Economics
Having examined environmental factors, we now turn to economic and demographic conditions and ask whether they help explain country-level differences in measles prevalence. Specifically, we seek to answer:

Is measles prevalence associated with country-level economic and demographic characteristics?

More specifically:

Does the impact of measles vary across countries?
Is the prevalence of measles associated with the economic prosperity of a country?

First, we will examine which countries have the highest and lowest number of measles cases per year. We chose to scale total cases per 100,000 people to provide better estimates on the prevalence of measles in a countries population.


Code
country_measles <- meta_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(avg_yearly_measles_cases = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_population = mean(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  mutate(cases_per_100k = (avg_yearly_measles_cases / avg_population) * 100000) |>
  filter(!is.na(cases_per_100k),
         is.finite(cases_per_100k),
         cases_per_100k != 0)

rank_type <- c("Highest", "Lowest")

get_rank <- function(rank_type) {
  if (rank_type == "Highest") {
    country_measles |>
      slice_max(cases_per_100k, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
      mutate(group = "5 Highest Cases per 100,000")
  } else {
    country_measles |>
      slice_min(cases_per_100k, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
      mutate(group = "5 Lowest Cases per 100,000")
  }
}

table_data <- map_dfr(rank_type, get_rank)

table_data |>
  gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
  cols_label(country = "Country",
             avg_yearly_measles_cases = "Avg. Yearly Measles Cases",
             avg_population = "Avg. Population",
             cases_per_100k = "Cases per 100,000 People") |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(avg_yearly_measles_cases,
                         avg_population,
                         cases_per_100k),
             decimals = 2) |>
  tab_header(title = "Countries with the Highest and Lowest Measles Cases per 100,000 People",
             subtitle = "Based on average yearly measles cases and average population") |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_row_groups()) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_title()) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_source_note(
                  source_note = md("*Table 3: Countries are ranked by average yearly measles cases per 100,000 people. The table compares the five countries with the highest rates and the five countries with the lowest rates.*")
)|>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )

We then examined the relationship between a country’s economic standing and its measles cases. Since graphing all 200 countries would produce a cluttered visualization, we focused on the five richest and five poorest countries and compared how their measles cases changed over time. These countries were identified using a Wikipedia page ranking countries by GDP (PPP) per capita.


Code
richest_5 <- c("Singapore", "Ireland", "Luxembourg", "Norway", "Qatar")
poorest_5 <- c("Burundi", "Central African Republic", "South Sudan", "Yemen", "Mozambique")

timeseries_df <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, 1),
  measles_per_100k = (measles_total / (population * 1000)) * 100000  ) |>
  filter(country %in% c(richest_5, poorest_5)) |>
  mutate(
    wealth_group = case_when(
      country %in% richest_5 ~ "Richest 5",
      country %in% poorest_5 ~ "Poorest 5"
    )
  )

timeseries_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = measles_per_100k, group = country, color = wealth_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Richest 5" = "steelblue4", "Poorest 5" = "firebrick3")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Adjusted Measles Rates Over Time (2012-2025)",
    subtitle = "Top 5 richest vs bottom 5 poorest countries",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Measles Cases per 100,000 People",
    color = "Group") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom")





Figure 3: Line plot of measles cases over time comparing five richest countries and five poorest countries from Wikipedia ranking.




From 2012 to 2025, the poorest five countries experienced higher measles case counts than the richest five countries. While measles cases in the richest group remained close to zero in the past 13 years, the poorest group showed repeated outbreak spikes, with large increases in more recent times. This suggests a strong association between GDP and measles cases.
While the time series plot compares measles rates between the richest and poorest country groups, it does not show the size of the economic difference between those groups. The following table will take a deeper dive into this discrepancy.


Code
gdppc_for_countries <- function(data, rich_countries, poor_countries) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      gdp_per_capita = (gdp / population) * 1000,
      group = case_when(
        country %in% rich_countries ~ "Richest 5",
        country %in% poor_countries ~ "Poorest 5")) |>
    filter(country %in% c(rich_countries, poor_countries)) |>
    group_by(group, country) |>
    arrange(desc(year), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    summarize(
      gdp_per_capita_usd = first(gdp_per_capita),
      .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(group, desc(gdp_per_capita_usd)) |>
    gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
    fmt_currency(
      columns = gdp_per_capita_usd,
      currency = "USD",
      decimals = 0) |>
    cols_label(
      country = "Country",
      gdp_per_capita_usd = "GDP per Capita (USD)") |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_title()) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_row_groups()) |>
    tab_header(
      title = md("**GDP per Capita of Selected Richest and Poorest Countries**"),
      subtitle = "Countries grouped using GDP per capita rankings") |>
    tab_source_note(
                    source_note = md("*Table 4: GDP per capita was calculated as GDP divided by population.
      GDP expressed in millions of USD, and population in thousands.*")) |>
    tab_options(table.width = pct(65))|>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
}

gdppc_for_countries(meta_data, richest_5, poorest_5)

It is important to note that because the countries were grouped using GDP per capita rankings, an economic gap between the two groups is expected. However, the size of this gap is not immediately visible from the time series plot alone. Based on the table, the selected richest countries have approximately 150 times higher GDP per capita than the selected poorest countries, highlighting the large economic difference between the groups.
Now, we will examine the relationship between GDP per capita and measles cases by country. Because our GDP data is taken at a single time for each country, we averaged measles cases per country, per year and plotted the average number of annual cases vs. GPD per capita. A Hexbin plot was also used as opposed to a scatterplot to better visualize the density of countries in certain GDP/case ratios.


Code
library(scales)

meta_data |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(annual_measles = sum(measles_total, na.rm=TRUE),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region.x),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(mean_annual_measles = mean(annual_measles),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region),
            .grpups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_annual_measles)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 20) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma_format()) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "blueviolet",
                      high = "goldenrod") +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country",
    x = "GDP per capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean annual measles cases (log scale)",
    fill = "Number of Countries",
    alt = paste(
      "Hexbin plot of countries comparing GDP per capita and mean annual measles
      cases.",
      "Both axes are on log scale.",
      "Contries are concentrated along a negatively correlated line, with many 
      contries having low GDP and high measles cases, while many others have low
      measles cases and high GDP."
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw() 





Figure 4. GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country.




Figure 4 shows that as GDP per capita increases, mean annual measles cases decrease. Richer countries, on average, report fewer cases — a pattern consistent with better vaccination coverage, healthcare access, and surveillance, though our cross-sectional data cannot distinguish between these explanations.


Data Model
For deeper analysis, we built a linear regression model to examine whether country characteristics are associated with measles prevalence. We chose to log transform the response variable, measles cases per 100,000 people, and the explanatory variables, GDP per capita, and number of tourists.


Code
linearreg_df <- meta_data |>
  select(
    country,
    year,
    gdp,
    population,
    measles_total,
    mean_temp,
    urban_population,
    tourists) |>
  mutate(

Results
The regression results reinforce, rather than contradict, the patterns seen earlier in the report. Log GDP per capita carries a strong negative coefficient: holding climate, tourism, and urbanization constant, wealthier countries report systematically lower measles rates per 100,000 — the same gradient that produced the divergent trajectories of the richest and poorest five countries in Figure 3 and the negatively-sloped band in Figure 4. Mean temperature is positively associated with measles, consistent with the regional density patterns in Figure 2 and the seasonal contrast between India and Indonesia in Table 1. The positive coefficient on log tourists is plausibly bidirectional: tourism creates importation pathways for measles, and tourism-heavy countries also tend to maintain stronger surveillance systems that detect more cases. Urban population shows a negative association, hinting that more concentrated populations may receive vaccination and clinical care more efficiently than dispersed ones.
Taken together, the three analyses — seasonal/regional, economic, and the joint regression — point to the same conclusion from different angles: measles burden is concentrated in lower-income, warmer countries, and over the 2012–2025 window the case-rate gap between rich and poor countries has widened rather than closed.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Data descriptions

World Health Organization (WHO)
We are exploring measles and rubella cases across the world. The data was collected and published through the World Health Organization (WHO) portal, but for ease of access scripts to access the data were provided by tidytuesday.
WHO has published monthly measles and rubella data from 193 countries from June 2012 to 2025. The data is provisional and is early case reports by WHO member countries collected using each countries national surveillance systems. The data contains several types of case confirmations (suspected, clinical, epidemiologically linked, and lab confirmed) as well as population count data in the yearly data set. The data was collected to monitor measles and rubella cases worldwide in order to support public health decision making and vaccination strategies.


Code

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(rvest)
library(here)
library(broom)

# reading in from github
cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')

# need to do some cleaning for the webscraping process
cases_month2 <- cases_month |>
  mutate(month_abb = recode_values(month, 
                        1 ~ "Jan",
                        2 ~ "Feb",
                        3 ~ "Mar",
                        4 ~ "Apr",
                        5 ~ "May",
                        6 ~ "Jun",
                        7 ~ "Jul",
                        8 ~ "Aug",
                        9 ~ "Sep",
                        10 ~ "Oct",
                        11 ~ "Nov",
                        12 ~ "Dec"),
      country_scrape = str_to_lower(country),
      country_scrape = str_replace_all(country_scrape, " ", "-"))




Weather and Climate Data
To investigate the relationship between seasonal patterns and measles cases, we’ve web scraped data provided by WeatherandClimate. WeatherAndClimate is an online climate zone and data finder for 80,000+ locations in the world based on Köppen climate classification. WeatherAndClimate does not disclose the exact source or data collection method. They provide historical records dating to 1980, but we will scrape mean monthly temperature and precipitation data across this time period. The data was collected to monitor Earth’s climate systems which have broad implications on socio-economic conditions.


Code
country_vec <- c(unique(cases_month2$country_scrape))

grab_weather <- function(country) {
   base_url <- "https://weatherandclimate.com/"
   climate_url <- str_c(base_url,country) 
   
   result <- tryCatch({
       read_html(climate_url)
     }, error = function(e) {
       message("Failed for ", country, e$message)
       NULL
     })
 
   if (is.null(result)) {
     return(NULL) 
   } else {
     result |>
       html_element(".tb6") |>
       html_table() |>
       pivot_longer(col = Jan:Dec, names_to = "month", values_to = "stat") |> 
       filter(Month %in% c("Average precipitation mm (inches)", "Daily mean °C (°F)")) |>
       select(!Year) |>
       pivot_wider(names_from = "Month", values_from = "stat") |>
       rename(mean_temp = 2,
          mean_precip = 3) |>
       mutate(mean_temp = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_temp, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             mean_precip = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_precip, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             country = country) #got the regex case from AI looks for values in the ()
 
   }
 }
 
 scraped_weather <- map(.x = country_vec, .f = grab_weather) |>
   bind_rows(.id = "id")

Country Demographic Dataset
The Country Demographic Datset is taken from API Ninja’s Country API, which provides geographic, demographic, and economic statistics about every country in the world. Each row represents a single country and each column is a statistic about that country, of which there are 187 countries included and over 40 different statistics.

  GET(url = base_url,

      query = list(name = country_name)) |>
  content("text", encoding = "utf-8") |>
  fromJSON() |>
  as.data.frame()
}

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Introduction
Measles is one of the most contagious diseases known and one of the most preventable: a two-dose vaccine costs roughly a dollar and offers near-lifetime protection. Even so, the past several years have seen a global resurgence. Routine immunization slipped during the COVID-19 pandemic, conflict and displacement disrupted vaccination campaigns in several regions, and outbreaks have re-emerged in countries that had previously eliminated the disease — raising concern that hard-won progress toward elimination is being lost.
This report combines three datasets — provisional WHO case reports, scraped historical climate data, and country-level economic and demographic indicators — to ask where measles is concentrated and which country-level conditions are most associated with its burden. We organize the analysis around two questions: how climate and geography relate to measles rates, and whether measles prevalence tracks economic and demographic differences between countries. We close with a regression model that brings these threads together and discuss what the combined evidence suggests for global measles control.

# load csv climate data
scraped_data <- read.csv(here("data", "weather-scrape.csv")) 

cases_month3 <- left_join(cases_month2, scraped_data, by = c("month_abb" = "month",  "country_scrape" = "country" ), 
                          relationship = "many-to-many")
meta_data <- left_join(cases_month3,
                       api_load,
                       by = c("country" = "name"),
                       relationship = "many-to-many")


Our final dataset is the three previous datasets joined together. The web-scraped data was joined onto the measles case data by month and by country. The API fetched data was joined onto the result of the previous join by country. Thus, we have a single dataset containing statistics on measles cases, countries, and environmental factors.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Research Questions

Climate, Geography and Measles Rates
Our first analysis aims to isolate trends in measles rates across climatic factors, natural resources and geography. We aim to answer the following question:

How do climate and geography relate to measles and rubella cases across the world?

And more specifically:

Are there seasonal patterns in measles outbreaks across different regions?
How are climate, natural resources and emissions related to measles/rubella cases?
How are total measles cases spatially distributed throughout the world’s regions?

To better understand the relationship between climate and measles/rubella cases, we will explore monthly mean lab confirmed measles/rubella cases in the northern and southern hemisphere. We will ignore changes across years, and focus on monthly variation. We will compare India and Indonesia, who are both in the top 10 countries with the worst measles outbreaks as of 2023.


Code
# Use of unmodified column, custom function
monthly_sums <- function(df, group, countries) {
  if (is.data.frame(df)) {
    df |>
      filter(country %in% c(countries)) |>
      group_by({{group}}, country) |>
      summarize(
                `Mean Measles Cases` = mean(measles_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                `Mean Rubella Cases` = mean(rubella_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                .groups = "drop") |>
      mutate(month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
      arrange(month_abb) |>
      pivot_longer(cols = c(`Mean Measles Cases`, `Mean Rubella Cases`),
                   names_to =  "Statistic",
                   values_to = "value") |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = month_abb,
                  values_from = value) |>
      group_by(country) |>
      gt() |>
      tab_style(
        style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
        locations = list(cells_column_labels(), cells_row_groups())) |> 
      tab_header(
        title = md("Mean Lab Confirmed Measles and Rubella Cases Per Month")) |>
      tab_source_note(
        source_note = md("*Table 1: Mean monthly lab-confirmed measles and rubella cases over the course of the study (2012-2025).*")) |>
      fmt_number(
        columns  = where(is.numeric),
        decimals = 1) |>
      data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
  } else {
  stop('Must input a dataframe')
  }
}

monthly_sums(df = meta_data, 
             group = month_abb,
            countries = c("Indonesia" , "India"))

From our analysis we see a strong relationship between month and measles/rubella cases. This confirms the long held notion of a northern hemisphere “flu season” during the winter. The relationship in Indonesia is more subtle and may be indicative of their tropical climate.
Next, we investigate how climate, natural resources and emissions are related to measles/rubella cases.


Code
library(corrr)
make_titles <- function(x){
  # expects a character or factor as an input
  stopifnot(is.character(x) | is.factor(x))
  
  # remove the units at the end of the name
  str_remove(x, pattern = "(g|mm)$") |> 
  # replace all _ with spaces
  str_replace_all(pattern = "_", replacement = " ") |> 
  # remove extra whitespace on left 
  str_trim(side = "both") |> 
  # convert the first letter of each word to upper case
  str_to_title()
}

meta_data |>
  select(month, measles_total, measles_epi_linked, rubella_total, 
         rubella_epi_linked, mean_temp, mean_precip, surface_area,
         forested_area, co2_emissions, threatened_species, internet_users) |>
  correlate(method = "pearson",
            use = "pairwise.complete.obs") |>
  rename(term1 = term) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = -term1,
               names_to = "term2",
               values_to = "correlation") |>
  filter(term1 != term2) |>
  mutate(
    across(.cols = c(term1, term2),
           .fns = ~ make_titles(.x)
           )
    ) |>
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = term1,
                       y = term2,
                       fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "",
       title = "Correlation Between Measles/Rubella Cases and Biological Factors",
       fill = "Correlation"
       ) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B")





Figure 1: Correlation matrix showing the relationship between climate, natural resources and emissions data and measles/rubella cases.




When comparing climate and natural resources, we see expected results such as a positive correlation between mean precipitation and forested area. We also see a positive correlation between CO₂ emissions and measles/rubella cases (Figure 1) — a surprising finding that likely reflects population and industrial scale rather than a direct biological link.
Finally, we investigate spatial differences in measles cases from last year and compare the distributions of total measles cases across world regions (Figure 2).


Code
regions_df <- cases_year |>

    mutate(measles_total = as.numeric(measles_total)) |>

      region = region_name,
      total_cases = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE)

}

The density ridge plot spans the full dataset (2012-2025) and so reflects long-term regional measles rates, which differ from the 2025 snapshot in Table 2. Notably, the regions with the largest 2025 share are not always those with the heaviest long-run distributions — a shift that points to outbreak dynamics a deeper temporal analysis could unpack.


Measles Cases and Economics
Having examined environmental factors, we now turn to economic and demographic conditions and ask whether they help explain country-level differences in measles prevalence. Specifically, we seek to answer:

Is measles prevalence associated with country-level economic and demographic characteristics?

More specifically:

Does the impact of measles vary across countries?
Is the prevalence of measles associated with the economic prosperity of a country?

First, we will examine which countries have the highest and lowest number of measles cases per year. We chose to scale total cases per 100,000 people to provide better estimates on the prevalence of measles in a countries population.


Code
country_measles <- meta_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(avg_yearly_measles_cases = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_population = mean(population, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  mutate(cases_per_100k = (avg_yearly_measles_cases / avg_population) * 100000) |>
  filter(!is.na(cases_per_100k),
         is.finite(cases_per_100k),
         cases_per_100k != 0)

rank_type <- c("Highest", "Lowest")

get_rank <- function(rank_type) {
  if (rank_type == "Highest") {
    country_measles |>
      slice_max(cases_per_100k, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
      mutate(group = "5 Highest Cases per 100,000")
  } else {
    country_measles |>
      slice_min(cases_per_100k, n = 5, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
      mutate(group = "5 Lowest Cases per 100,000")
  }
}

table_data <- map_dfr(rank_type, get_rank)

table_data |>
  gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
  cols_label(country = "Country",
             avg_yearly_measles_cases = "Avg. Yearly Measles Cases",
             avg_population = "Avg. Population",
             cases_per_100k = "Cases per 100,000 People") |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(avg_yearly_measles_cases,
                         avg_population,
                         cases_per_100k),

)|>

We then examined the relationship between a country’s economic standing and its measles cases. Since graphing all 200 countries would produce a cluttered visualization, we focused on the five richest and five poorest countries and compared how their measles cases changed over time. These countries were identified using a Wikipedia page ranking countries by GDP (PPP) per capita.


Code
richest_5 <- c("Singapore", "Ireland", "Luxembourg", "Norway", "Qatar")
poorest_5 <- c("Burundi", "Central African Republic", "South Sudan", "Yemen", "Mozambique")

timeseries_df <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, 1),
  measles_per_100k = (measles_total / (population * 1000)) * 100000  ) |>
  filter(country %in% c(richest_5, poorest_5)) |>
  mutate(
    wealth_group = case_when(
      country %in% richest_5 ~ "Richest 5",
      country %in% poorest_5 ~ "Poorest 5"
    )
  )

timeseries_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = measles_per_100k, group = country, color = wealth_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Richest 5" = "steelblue4", "Poorest 5" = "firebrick3")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Adjusted Measles Rates Over Time (2012-2025)",
    subtitle = "Top 5 richest vs bottom 5 poorest countries",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Measles Cases per 100,000 People",
    color = "Group") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom")





Figure 3: Line plot of measles cases over time comparing five richest countries and five poorest countries from Wikipedia ranking.




From 2012 to 2025, the poorest five countries experienced higher measles case counts than the richest five countries. While measles cases in the richest group remained close to zero in the past 13 years, the poorest group showed repeated outbreak spikes, with large increases in more recent times. This suggests a strong association between GDP and measles cases.
While the time series plot compares measles rates between the richest and poorest country groups, it does not show the size of the economic difference between those groups. The following table will take a deeper dive into this discrepancy.


Code
gdppc_for_countries <- function(data, rich_countries, poor_countries) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      gdp_per_capita = (gdp / population) * 1000,
      group = case_when(
        country %in% rich_countries ~ "Richest 5",
        country %in% poor_countries ~ "Poorest 5")) |>
    filter(country %in% c(rich_countries, poor_countries)) |>
    group_by(group, country) |>
    arrange(desc(year), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    summarize(
      gdp_per_capita_usd = first(gdp_per_capita),
      .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(group, desc(gdp_per_capita_usd)) |>
    gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
    fmt_currency(
      columns = gdp_per_capita_usd,
      currency = "USD",
      decimals = 0) |>
    cols_label(
      country = "Country",
      gdp_per_capita_usd = "GDP per Capita (USD)") |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_title()) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
    tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_row_groups()) |>
    tab_header(
      title = md("**GDP per Capita of Selected Richest and Poorest Countries**"),
      subtitle = "Countries grouped using GDP per capita rankings") |>
    tab_source_note(
                    source_note = md("*Table 4: GDP per capita was calculated as GDP divided by population.
      GDP expressed in millions of USD, and population in thousands.*")) |>

}

Now, we will examine the relationship between GDP per capita and measles cases by country. Because our GDP data is taken at a single time for each country, we averaged measles cases per country, per year and plotted the average number of annual cases vs. GPD per capita. A Hexbin plot was also used as opposed to a scatterplot to better visualize the density of countries in certain GDP/case ratios.


Code
library(scales)

meta_data |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(annual_measles = sum(measles_total, na.rm=TRUE),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region.x),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(mean_annual_measles = mean(annual_measles),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region),
            .grpups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_annual_measles)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 20) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma_format()) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "blueviolet",
                      high = "goldenrod") +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country",
    x = "GDP per capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean annual measles cases (log scale)",
    fill = "Number of Countries",
    alt = paste(
      "Hexbin plot of countries comparing GDP per capita and mean annual measles
      cases.",
      "Both axes are on log scale.",
      "Contries are concentrated along a negatively correlated line, with many 
      contries having low GDP and high measles cases, while many others have low
      measles cases and high GDP."
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw() 





Figure 4. GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country.




Figure 4 shows that as GDP per capita increases, mean annual measles cases decrease. Richer countries, on average, report fewer cases — a pattern consistent with better vaccination coverage, healthcare access, and surveillance, though our cross-sectional data cannot distinguish between these explanations.


Data Model
For deeper analysis, we built a linear regression model to examine whether country characteristics are associated with measles prevalence. We chose to log transform the response variable, measles cases per 100,000 people, and the explanatory variables, GDP per capita, and number of tourists.


Code
linearreg_df <- meta_data |>
  select(
    country,
    year,
    gdp,
    population,
    measles_total,
    mean_temp,
    urban_population,
    tourists) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_per_capita = gdp / population,
    measles_rate_100k = (measles_total / population) * 100000,
    log_gdp_per_capita = log(gdp_per_capita),
    log_measles_rate_100k = log(measles_rate_100k + 1),
    log_tourists = log(tourists)) 
model <-  lm(
    log_measles_rate_100k ~ log_gdp_per_capita + mean_temp + log_tourists + urban_population, 
    data = linearreg_df) 

model |>
  tidy() |>
  mutate(term = recode_values(term,
                              "(Intercept)" ~ "Intercept",
                              "log_gdp_per_capita" ~ "Log GDP per Capita",
                              "mean_temp" ~ "Mean Temperature",
                              "log_tourists" ~ "Log Number of Tourists",
                              "urban_population" ~ "Urban Population")) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, std.error, statistic),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  fmt_scientific(columns = p.value) |>
  cols_label(
    term = md("**Predictor**"),
    estimate = md("**Estimate**"),
    std.error = md("**Std. Error**"),
    statistic = md("**t-value**"),
    p.value = md("**p-value**")
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Regression Model Results**"),
    subtitle = "Predicting logged measles case rate per 100,000"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
                  source_note = md("*Table 5: Linear regression using GDP per capita, mean temperature, number of tourists, and urban population as predictors.*"))|>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"

Results
The regression results reinforce, rather than contradict, the patterns seen earlier in the report. Log GDP per capita carries a strong negative coefficient: holding climate, tourism, and urbanization constant, wealthier countries report systematically lower measles rates per 100,000 — the same gradient that produced the divergent trajectories of the richest and poorest five countries in Figure 3 and the negatively-sloped band in Figure 4. Mean temperature is positively associated with measles, consistent with the regional density patterns in Figure 2 and the seasonal contrast between India and Indonesia in Table 1. The positive coefficient on log tourists is plausibly bidirectional: tourism creates importation pathways for measles, and tourism-heavy countries also tend to maintain stronger surveillance systems that detect more cases. Urban population shows a negative association, hinting that more concentrated populations may receive vaccination and clinical care more efficiently than dispersed ones.
Taken together, the three analyses — seasonal/regional, economic, and the joint regression — point to the same conclusion from different angles: measles burden is concentrated in lower-income, warmer countries, and over the 2012–2025 window the case-rate gap between rich and poor countries has widened rather than closed.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Limitations
Several caveats apply to these findings.

Surveillance heterogeneity. WHO case data are provisional and depend on each country’s surveillance capacity. Under-reporting is most likely in exactly the low-income settings where our model estimates the highest burden, so the true rich–poor gap may be wider than what we observe.
Time-invariant covariates. GDP, urbanization, tourism, and other API Ninjas variables come from a single snapshot and are joined as constants to thirteen years of monthly case data, masking real within-country change.
Repeated observations. The regression treats country-year rows as independent, but each country contributes up to thirteen rows. Standard errors are likely optimistic; a mixed-effects specification with country as a random intercept would be a more honest model.
Climate scrape coverage. The web scrape failed for 19 of 173 countries, so the climate analyses are biased toward countries with parseable WeatherAndClimate pages.
Confounding. All relationships are observational. Vaccination coverage, conflict exposure, and healthcare-system quality are not directly in the data and are plausibly confounded with both GDP and measles rates. The positive correlation between CO₂ emissions and measles cases in Figure 1, in particular, is more likely to reflect total population and industrial scale — large populous countries report both more emissions and more cases — than a direct biological link.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Conclusion
The global measles picture from 2012 to 2025 is one of widening inequality rather than steady progress. Climate and seasonality help explain when outbreaks happen, but economic and demographic conditions explain where they take hold and how severe they become. Across our analyses, GDP per capita is the strongest country-level correlate of measles burden, with warmer average temperatures and lower urbanization compounding the effect. None of these relationships are causal, but together they sharpen where intervention is most needed. Future work should incorporate vaccination-coverage data directly, model the temporal dynamics that our cross-sectional country variables obscure, and revisit the CO₂–measles association we flag above with measures less correlated with country size.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Introduction
Measles is one of the most contagious diseases known and one of the most preventable: a two-dose vaccine costs roughly a dollar and offers near-lifetime protection. Even so, the past several years have seen a global resurgence. Routine immunization slipped during the COVID-19 pandemic, conflict and displacement disrupted vaccination campaigns in several regions, and outbreaks have re-emerged in countries that had previously eliminated the disease — raising concern that hard-won progress toward elimination is being lost.
This report combines three datasets — provisional WHO case reports, scraped historical climate data, and country-level economic and demographic indicators — to ask where measles is concentrated and which country-level conditions are most associated with its burden. We organize the analysis around two questions: how climate and geography relate to measles rates, and whether measles prevalence tracks economic and demographic differences between countries. We close with a regression model that brings these threads together and discuss what the combined evidence suggests for global measles control.

title: Measles cases across the world
text:
Conclusion
The global measles picture from 2012 to 2025 is one of widening inequality rather than steady progress. Climate and seasonality help explain when outbreaks happen, but economic and demographic conditions explain where they take hold and how severe they become. Across our analyses, GDP per capita is the strongest country-level correlate of measles burden, with warmer average temperatures and lower urbanization compounding the effect. None of these relationships are causal, but together they sharpen where intervention is most needed. Future work should incorporate vaccination-coverage data directly, model the temporal dynamics that our cross-sectional country variables obscure, and revisit the CO₂–measles association we flag above with measures less correlated with country size.
